segments.s260-g41: ور introduces a conditional ('if'); ایدون (lit. 'it comes/appears') is an archaic poetic formula meaning 'if it happens' or 'should it come to pass'. که introduces the subordinate clause 'that...'; ترسد is present/subjunctive-style 'he fears' with همی adding a continuous/emphatic aspect ('still/indeed'); از کمین marks the source ('from an ambush'). Together this clause means 'if it should come to pass that he fears from an ambush.'
segments.s260-g42: ز (poetic form of از) marks origin/agent 'from' and governs the following noun phrase; جنگ سواران is a genitive/compound-like sequence 'war–horsemen' (i.e. cavalry engaged in battle); و links to مردان کین 'and the men of vengeance/hatred' — کین is an attributive noun meaning 'vengeance' giving 'vengeful men'. The whole phrase specifies who the ambush is by.
segments.s260-g43: به من marks the beneficiary ('to/for me'); داد here functions verbally/nominally in an older idiom with باید ('it is necessary to give' / 'you must give'); باید is the necessity modal ('must'); سواری هزار places the object 'a thousand horsemen' after the modal phrase — word order is somewhat inverted and poetic but yields 'a thousand horsemen must be given to me.'
segments.s260-g44: گزین is an imperative/infinitival notion 'choose' or 'select' attached to من (here 'for me' or 'by me' depending on reading); اندرخور is an adjective meaning 'suitable' or 'worthy of'; کارزار is the domain 'battle'/'battlefield'. Together this is a request/command: 'select for me those suitable for the battle.'
segments.s260-g45: برآریم is 1st person plural present-subjunctive/optative 'we raise/let us raise' (poetic mood); گرد here means 'dust' (a frequent epic formula 'raise dust' to indicate a charge or rout); از governs the source 'from'; کمینگاهشان is the possessive compound 'their ambush/place of lying in wait'. The clause thus means 'we will raise dust from their ambushes.'
segments.s260-g46: سرافشان (here adjectival/participial) carries the notion of 'scattering / making to flee' (literally 'scatter/loose the heads or hair' in poetry); کنیم is the 1st person plural present 'we do/make' giving 'we will cause/scatter'; از بر is a prepositional combination 'from before' or 'out of the presence of'; ماهشان ('their moon') is a poetic epithet—'moon' used metaphorically for a leader or beautiful chief. The whole clause means 'and we will scatter them before their leader.'
segments.s260-g47: ز here means 'from/because of' or 'on account of' and links to گفتار 'speech/words' (object); بیژن is the proper name in genitive ('Bijan's words'); بخندید is past tense 'laughed'; گیو (Giv) is the subject placed after the verb in the poetic word order. Literally: 'From Bijan's speech Giv laughed.'
segments.s260-g48: بسی is an adverbial intensifier here ('very/much'); آفرین is 'praise/acclamation' (an exclamation or noun); کرد is the past verb 'did/made' so 'gave praise'; بر introduces the beneficiary/target ('upon'); پور is 'son' (used genealogically), and نیو is a name (here the father's name). Together: 'he gave much praise to Niu's son.'
segments.s260-g1: به‌روز چهارم is a temporal prepositional phrase meaning 'on the fourth day' (به‌روز + ordinal). ز (poetic 'from') + پیش ('before/in front of') together indicate 'from the front' or 'out of the vanguard'; سپاه is the noun 'army' governed by those prepositions. Word order places the time phrase first and then the locative/agent phrase.
segments.s260-g2: بشد here functions as a past/resultive verb ('it happened/they came'), introducing the main action. The two proper names بیژن گیو are listed in apposition (no conjunction), a common epic device meaning 'Bijan and Giv'. تا before قلبگاه means 'as far as/up to' indicating extent; قلبگاه is a figurative 'heart/center' (here of the battle).
segments.s260-g3: به پیش پدر is a prepositional phrase 'before/in the presence of the father'. شد is the past/coproductive verb 'became/were' used here as a copula to state condition: 'their garments became torn.' همه جامه چاک places the quantifier 'all' before 'garments' and the adjective/participle چاک ('torn') follows the noun, giving 'all their garments torn.'
segments.s260-g4: همی is an older/poetic adverb indicating continuity or ongoing action ('kept on / indeed'). بآسمان + بر expresses direction 'toward/to the sky' and پراگند خاک is a noun phrase 'scattered dust' (پراگند modifying خاک). The clause lacks an explicit finite throwing-verb in modern syntax; the action is implied (they scattered/they cast dust), with همی marking the continuing action.
segments.s260-g5: بدو گفت is 'he said to him': بدو (to him/unto him) is a prepositional clitic placed before the verb; گفت is the past of گفتن 'said'. کای is an archaic vocative 'O' used in epic address; باب کارآزمای is an epithet/title in apposition (Bab + کارآزمای 'tester/trier of deeds').
segments.s260-g6: چه داری is an interrogative 'what do you have / what is it that you have' but functions idiomatically here as 'what is the reason/what makes you...'. چنین 'thus/so' modifies خیره 'staring/astounded'. ما را is the object 'us' with را (definite/object marker). بپای is the present/habitual form of پاییدن/پاییدن (with the prefix ب- indicating ongoing or present aspect), meaning 'stand/abide' — so the phrase asks why he stands there fixed, staring at them.
segments.s260-g7: به پنجم is a temporal phrase 'on the fifth'; فراز آمد is a verbal phrase 'rose to a summit / came up high' (فراز = height/top, آمد = came). این روزگار is the subject ('this time/period'); the verb آمد precedes the subject here, which is acceptable classical/poetic word order.
segments.s260-g8: شب و روز 'night and day' is an adverbial phrase qualifying the following predicate. آسایش آموزگار is a nominal predicate 'a teacher/bring-er of rest/peace' — آموزگار used metaphorically: there is no explicit copula (است) because Persian often omits it in such predicative phrases, especially in poetry.
segments.s260-g17: به لاون introduces a place (به + place) meaning 'to/at Lavān' in classical usage. که introduces a relative clause 'where/that', followed by چندان 'so many/that much' modifying پسر 'sons/young men'. کشته is a past participle 'killed/slain' and دید is the main verb 'he saw' (past tense) that takes the whole relative clause as its object.
segments.s260-g18: سر بخت ایرانیان is a genitival phrase 'the Iranians' bāxt/head of fortune' (سر here = chief/head). گشته is the perfect/participial sense 'has become/been turned' and دید is again the reporting verb 'he saw' (past). The sequence thus reports that he perceived the Iranians' fortune to have been overturned.
segments.s260-g19: جگر here is figurative 'heart/courage'. خسته گشته است uses گشته + است to form a perfect/resultant state 'has become tired'. و links the clauses; گم‌کرده راه is a reduced adjectival phrase 'lost the way' (گم‌کرده = 'lost', modifying راه 'way').
segments.s260-g20: نخواهد here is the negative future/impersonal 'will not' (classical usage of خواه in negative form), followed by the subordinating که + verb بیند 'to see' — so 'he will not see'. همی is a classical adverbial particle indicating continuity or emphasis ('anymore/any longer' in context). رزمگاه is the object 'battlefield.'
segments.s260-g21: به پیرانش 'to/for his elders' (پیران with enclitic -ش 'his'). بر چشم باید فگند is an idiomatic classical sequence: باید 'it is necessary/must' governs the action verb فگند (an archaic form of فکند 'to cast/throw'), with بر چشم 'upon the eye' indicating where the gaze or action is directed. The archaic verb form فگند appears as an infinitival/obligative complement to باید.
segments.s260-g22: نهادست is a perfect/resultant stative 'is set/placed' (here 'is turned/sets'); سر is literal 'head' (physical head/face). سوی 'toward' introduces the direction, and کوه بلند 'the high mountain' is the goal. Word order is subject (implicit: 'his head') + predicate (نهادست) + direction phrase.
segments.s260-g23: سپهدار 'commander' is followed by کو used in classical Persian as a relative/'who' or 'where' introducing the qualifier. ناشمرده 'uncounted/innumerable' modifies سپاه 'army/host'. The phrase functions as a descriptive relative: 'a commander whose host is uncounted.'
segments.s260-g24: ستاره شمارد is a present/habitual third-person 'he counts the stars' (شمارد = 'counts'). همی is the classical continuous/emphatic particle 'continually/indeed'. گرد ماه literally 'around the moon' (گرد = 'around/about'); together the clause says he counts the stars (constantly) about/around the moon — a poetic image of counting celestial bodies.
segments.s260-g25: تو بشناس is an imperative address — 'you, know' — followed by a subordinate clause introduced by the archaic fused particle کاندر (= که در) meaning 'that/in which'. تنش is 'تن' + possessive suffix ش 'his' ('his body'). نیست precedes the noun خون for poetic inversion: literally 'that in his body is not blood' = 'there is no blood in his body' (blood here = courage).
segments.s260-g26: Poetic word order places the verb شد ('became') before the adverbial phrase and subject: the normal prose order would be جنگاوران او از جنگ زبون شدند — 'his warriors became abject from the fighting.' از جنگ is the causal/agent phrase 'from/through battle'; زبون is an adjectival predicate 'abject, hapless.'
segments.s260-g27: شگفت here functions as the noun 'wonder/marvel' with نیست negating it: 'there is no wonder.' از + epithet + name ('از جهاندیده گودرز') gives the source/agent sense 'coming from the world‑wise Goudarz' (جهاندیده is an epithet placed before the name).
segments.s260-g28: که introduces the explanatory clause. او را uses the object/ topicalizing marker را after the pronoun to foreground 'him'; روان خود = 'his own soul/spirit' (خود emphatic/possessive). بر این مرز نیست is a locative negation: 'is not upon this frontier' — i.e. his spirit/morale is not present here.
segments.s260-g29: شگفت آید مرا is a fixed idiom 'it astonishes/surprises me'; از تو gives the cause/ground 'because of you' or 'about you'. مرا is the dative 'to me' and is immediately followed by the vocative ای پدر 'O father' — a poetic attachment.
segments.s260-g30: که introduces the content clause. شیر ژیان is a compound epithet 'lion of life' (i.e. a heroic/lively lion) where ژیان ('life') modifies شیر. از تو 'from you' marks the source of the seeking; جوید is a present idea 'seeks' (3rd person), and هنر is the object 'skill/virtue.' Word order is poetic but straightforward: 'that the lion of life seeks skill from you.'
segments.s260-g31: دو لشکر is the plural subject 'two armies.' همی is a classical/poetic continuous particle meaning 'always/continually.' بر تو دارند چشم literally 'have (their) eye upon you' (دارند used as 'keep/hold'); this is an idiom meaning 'they watch you' or 'their eyes are on you.'
segments.s260-g32: This is a sequence of imperatives addressed to the addressee. یکی تیز کن = 'make one (of them) sharp' or 'sharpen one (eye/point)' (یکی used distributively/partitive). مغز here stands for 'mind/wits' (sharpen your intellect). و بفروز خشم is another imperative 'and kindle anger' (بفروز = light/kindle, خشم = anger) so the line commands focus, sharpened mind, and roused wrath.
segments.s260-g57: ببریم is the 1st-person-plural present-subjunctive / jussive of بردن (lit. 'let us take' or 'we will take'), so the verb governs the following direct object. از او = 'from him' (preposition + pronoun). مهر و پیوند پاک is a coordinated noun phrase 'love and a pure bond' with پاک (postpositive adjective) qualifying the whole phrase; Persian often places adjectives after the noun in classical verse.
segments.s260-g58: This is a copular clause with a somewhat compressed classical word order. پدرش = 'his father.' آب دریا = 'the water of the sea' (genitive relationship expressed by noun juxtaposition rather than an explicit ezāfe in poetry). بود is the past-form copula 'was.' مام خاک is an elliptical apposition 'his mother (was) the earth' — in classical Persian the copula can be omitted for the second predicate, so the meaning is supplied from بود.
segments.s260-g59: ولیکن = 'but' (conjunction). تو‌ای is a vocative address 'you — O ...', with ای marking the vocative. پور 'son/descendant' is the vocative noun and چیره سخن ('skilled in speech') is an adjectival phrase qualifying the addressee; classical Persian freely places adjectives after or before the noun in poetry.
segments.s260-g60: زبان = 'tongue' used metonymically for speech/uttering. بر here is a directional/prepositional element 'up/forward' used with a coming verb. نیا is an archaic negative imperative form (from آمدن) meaning 'do not bring/raise' (i.e. 'do not put forth'). The second clause بر گشاده مکن repeats the prohibition with مکن 'do not do' and گشاده 'open' (postpositive adjective), so together they mean 'do not thrust forth your tongue; do not open it (speak) openly.'
segments.s260-g61: که here introduces an explanatory clause ('for' / 'because' / 'that'). او = 'he.' کاردیدست is an adjectival/predicative formation meaning 'experienced' (کاردیده in later Persian); the -ست ending is the archaic/poetic copula. و links the second predicate داناترست, a comparative 'more learned/wise' (داناتر + ـست as copula).
segments.s260-g62: بر این لشکر = 'over this army/host' where بر functions as 'over/in command of.' نامور is a postpositive adjective 'renowned' modifying لشکر. مهترست is 'he is the chief' (مهتر = chief/elder; -ست = copula), so the whole phrase predicates his rank over that famed host.
segments.s260-g63: کسی 'someone' followed by the relative/archaic connector کو ('who') introduces a relative clause. بود is the past copula 'was' (here giving the sense 'has been' / 'is one who has'). سوده (literally 'profited' or 'advantageous') modifies کارزار 'battle', so the phrase means 'someone who has had success/profit in battle.' Note the classical, somewhat inverted order.
segments.s260-g64: نباید is an impersonal modal 'it is not necessary / should not / need not' and governs the following nominal phrase. به هر کارش = 'for/to every one of his deeds' (به = 'for', هر = 'every', کار + ش = 'his deed'). آموزگار = 'teacher'; the clause therefore says there is no need for a teacher for each of his actions.
segments.s260-g33: کنون (adverb) opens the clause = 'now'; چون is the subordinating conjunction here meaning 'when'; the phrase جهان گرم و روشن هوا places adjectives after their nouns in classical Persian (جهان گرم و روشن = 'the world warm and bright') and هِوا (air) is the noun that will be governed by the following verb. The clause is left incomplete here because the verb appears in the next segment.
segments.s260-g34: بگیرد is the present/subjunctive-ish verbal form used in classical Persian to mark an event that 'takes hold' or 'begins'; همی is the old continuous/progressive particle (here reinforcing habitual or immediate action). The verb phrase is followed by the noun phrase رزم لشکر نوا ('the army's war-tune' or 'battle-melody'), so the clause reads roughly 'the war-melody of the host takes hold' = 'the war-tune arises/starts.'
segments.s260-g35: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'; این روزگار خوشی is a noun phrase 'this time/age of happiness' with adjective خوشی following روزگار. بگذرد is the present/subjunctive/simple verb 'passes' (literally 'may/does pass'), so the whole segment means 'when this happy time passes.'
segments.s260-g36: Another temporal clause introduced by چو 'when'; پولاد ('steel') + روی ('face/surface') form a genitive/possessive notion 'the face/surface of steel' and زمین is 'the earth/on the ground' governed by the verb that follows. بفسرد here is intended in the poetic sense 'become cold' (classical usage), so the clause means 'when the steel's face on the ground grows cold.'
segments.s260-g37: چو 'when' introduces the condition; بر نیزه‌ها is a locative expression 'upon the spears' (بر = on/upon + نیزه‌ها = spears). گردد (گردد) is the classical/poetic present of شدن 'become', so 'گردد افسرده چنگ' means 'the harp becomes afflicted/sorrowful' — adjective افسرده follows the noun it describes. Note the inverted order (verb before adjective) is typical in poetry.
segments.s260-g38: پس here is 'then/thereupon'; پشت تیغ آید is literal 'the sword comes at (one's) back' (پشت = rear/behind, تیغ = sword, آید = comes). و پیش سنگ continues in parallel: و = and, پیش = before/in front, سنگ = stone. The sequence gives contrasting directions of attack: sword from the rear, stone from the front.
segments.s260-g39: که here introduces a relative/subordinate clause 'who/that'; آید = 'comes'; ز گردان = 'from the troop/rank' (ز = from, گردان = troop/unit); به پیش سپاه = 'to the front of the army' (به پیش = to the front, سپاه = host/army). The whole segment means 'who comes from the ranks to the front of the army.'
segments.s260-g40: Another clause introduced by که; آورد is the past/present stem 'brings' or 'causes', and گیرد (here) is the verb 'take/seize' — together poetic: 'who brings and seizes'; ب + دین + رزمگاه is the prepositional phrase 'to this battlefield' (badīn/bedīn = 'to this' in older usage), though the segmentation lists ب and دین separately: ب = to/into, دین in this position functions as part of the demonstrative 'this' (no matching sense in the supplied list, so treated as part of the idiom). رزمگاه = battlefield/contest-place.
segments.s260-g9: The clause begins with the negative particle نه introducing a correlative neither-clause. خورشید is the grammatical subject ('the sun'); شمشیر گردان is a compound-like phrase meaning 'sword‑brandishing' (گردان as participial/revolving here, describing those who wield swords). بدید is the archaic/past form of دیدن ('saw') in third person singular; the whole clause reads 'neither did the sun see the sword‑brandishing (ranks)'.
segments.s260-g10: This is the second member of the neither…nor construction, again introduced by نه. گردی ('a speck/dust') is the notional subject; به روی هوا is a prepositional phrase 'onto/into the air' (به = into/towards, روی = 'upon/on'); بردمید is a past passive/impersonal sense 'was carried up / rose' (here 'was lifted' or 'rose into the air'). So: 'nor did a speck of dust rise into the air.'
segments.s260-g11: سواران is the topic 'the horsemen'. The phrase به خفتان uses به + a verbal/nominal form of خفتن to mean 'in sleep / asleep' (elliptical copula: 'were asleep'). و خود اندرون links with 'and he himself inside' — خود اندرون is a short clause meaning 'he himself was inside' with the copula omitted (typical in poetry).
segments.s260-g12: یکی را uses the indefinite 'one / not a single one' with the object marker را (here idiomatic). The core is 'به رگ بر نجنبید خون' where خون ('blood') appears after the verb (poetic word order); نجنبید is the negative past 'did not stir/move', بر + رگ = 'in/at the vein' ('in the vein' = 'in his veins'). The whole clause means 'not a single man's blood stirred in his vein(s)'.
segments.s260-g13: به ایران is a locative 'in Iran'. پس از رستم is a temporal/relative phrase 'after Rostam' (پس = 'after' here, combined with از). نامدار is a postpositive adjective 'renowned / famed' modifying the implied person. Together the phrase sets the frame 'in Iran, after the famed Rostam'.
segments.s260-g14: نبودی is the past negative/existential 'there was not / there existed not' (archaic past copula). چو here means 'like' (a simile). The phrase چو گودرز دیگر سوار = 'like Goudarz, another rider' — with دیگر سوار 'another/other horseman'. So the clause asserts absence of any horseman comparable to Goudarz after Rostam.
segments.s260-g15: چینن (archaic چینن/چنین) = 'thus'. تا introduces a temporal subordinate 'until'. بیامد is the past 'he came / he returned'. ز جنگ = 'from the war'; پشن (dialectal/poetic) functions as 'back' or 'returning'—so together 'until he returned from the war.' The clause uses classical word order and an archaic adverb.
segments.s260-g16: از آن 'from that' (referring forward to some hardship); کشتن here is a noun-like use 'that killing/slaughter'. و links the two causes; رزمگاه گشن is a noun + postpositive adjective: 'the battlefield (رزمگاه) hungry' i.e. 'the hunger of the battlefield' or 'the hungry battlefield'. The phrase as a whole gives causes/conditions: 'worn by that slaughter and by the hunger of the battlefield.'
segments.s260-g49: به دادار گفت is a simple clause with the prepositional phrase (به دادار, “to the Creator”) placed before the past verb گفت (“said”) for emphasis — a common poetic order. از تو دارم سپاس literally reads “from you I have thanks” and uses از + pronoun to mean “for/thanks to you,” with دارم (present/simple possession) taking سپاس as its nominal object.
segments.s260-g50: This clause gives the gift: subject-to-verb-to-object order is compressed in poetry. تو (you) + دادی (you gave, past) is followed by the object phrase مرا/م را (split here as م + را) marking the recipient (“to me”) and پور نیکی‌شناس is the thing given — پور (son) modified by the adjective/compound نیکی‌شناس (“one who recognizes goodness” or “grateful/knowing good”).
segments.s260-g51: همش هوش دادی literally “you gave all (of) his wit” — همش (= entirely/all of it) modifies هوش (intellect). The clause continues with و هم (and also) + زور (strength). کین follows زور: in classical poetic usage کین can mean hostile feeling or force connected with enmity; here it accompanies زور as a quality given (the line strings qualities together).
segments.s260-g52: شناسای is a participial/adjectival form meaning “knowing/recognizer (of).” It governs the following nouns: هر کار “every deed/affair” (کار here = deed) and جویای دین “seeking religion” — جویای is another participle meaning “in search of/striving for,” and دین is used in the sense “religion.” Together these phrases enumerate intellectual and moral qualities.
segments.s260-g53: The verb phrase begins with the direction phrase به من (to me) followed by the past verb بازگشت (“returned”); the subject این دلاور جوان (“this valiant young man”) comes after the verb, a normal poetic inversion. بازگشت here is used as a verb (he returned) rather than the noun.
segments.s260-g54: چنان چون (both meaning “such/like”) form an emphatic comparative “just as/so that,” followed by بود (was) and the noun phrase بچه پهلوان (“a child/young champion”). The sequence places the simile after the return clause: “he returned to me, such as a young champion was.”
segments.s260-g55: چنین گفت introduces direct speech (“thus said”), مر is the enclitic-to-me (“to me”), and جفت را names the pair (the two animals/partners) as the speaker; را marks the definite object/emphatic phrase that follows, نره شیر, literally “the male lion.” The clause sets up reported speech or a statement by the pair.
segments.s260-g56: This is a subordinate clause introduced by که (“that/which/for”), stating a condition about فرزند ما (our child). ماگر is a conjunctive/conditional particle meaning “if not” or “unless”; نباشد is the negative present/subjunctive form of بودن (“to be”) used here in a conditional sense (“were not/is not”); دلیر is the predicate adjective “brave.” Together: “that our child, if he were not brave...” (the sentence continues in the next lines).
segments.s260-g65: سواران ما‌گر begins with the subject phrase 'our riders' (سواران ما) followed by the conditional particle ماگر meaning 'if/except that'. The predicate is the verbless clause 'به بار اندرند' in which اندرند (archaic present 3pl) is the copula/verb 'they are' placed at the end; به بار literally 'in/with a load' means 'laden with baggage'. Overall: 'If our horsemen are laden.'
segments.s260-g66: نه negates the following clause. ترکان is the subject 'the Turks' and به رنگ و نگار is a prepositional/locative phrase 'in color and ornament' describing their dress or appearance. اندرند again is the archaic present 3pl copula 'they are' at the end; the whole clause means 'the Turks are not (given to) color and ornament' (i.e. not showily dressed).
segments.s260-g67: همه شوربختند is a simple nominal clause 'all are wretched' with شوربختند as the predicate (pl.). The conjunction و links a participial description: برگشته سر literally 'head turned/turned-back' — برگشته is a past participle 'turned/returned' modifying سر; the pronoun is understood, so it reads 'and (their) heads are turned' or 'they have turned their heads (i.e. retreated).'
segments.s260-g68: Here همۀ begins a parallel predication: 'all [are] ...' with the copula omitted and understood. دیده 'eye/eyes' is a substantive used collectively, modified by the prefixed adjective پرخون ('full of blood', where پر- = 'full of'). The second coordinate خسته جگر places the adjective خسته before the noun جگر ('weary liver/heart'), another nominal description. The omission of a verb is normal: '[they have] eyes full of blood and weary hearts.'
segments.s260-g69: همی خواهد is a verbal phrase where همی (an emphatic/continuous adverb) combines with خواهد (3sg present of 'to want/need') — literally 'keeps wanting' or 'requires'. این باب کارآزمای is the subject noun phrase 'this matter/topic of trial/testing' (باب = 'topic/section', کارآزمای = 'testing/experimental' or 'trial'). Word order places the verb before the full NP for poetic emphasis, but the meaning is 'this matter calls for/needs...'.
segments.s260-g70: که introduces a subordinate clause 'that...' with ترکان as the subject. به جنگ اندر آرند پای is an idiomatic sequence: به جنگ 'into battle', پای 'foot', and اندر آرند (compound of اندر + آرد in archaic plural form آرند) together express 'set foot in / enter into battle.' The verb (آرند) is final and agrees with the plural subject; overall: 'that the Turks enter into the fight.'
segments.s260-g71: پس is a temporal/conclusive 'then'. پشتشان 'their rear/backs' contains the pronominal suffix -شان. دور ماند is a two-word verbal phrase: دور 'far' + ماند (past of ماندن) 'remained', so 'their rear remained far (away)'. ز کوه is the prepositional 'from the mountain' (ز is the poetic form of از). The clause states that their support/back was left distant from the mountain.
segments.s260-g72: In this terse, poetic sequence برد functions as a noun 'victory' (poetic word order places it before the noun it belongs to), followed by لشکر 'army/host' which is modified by the adjectives کینه‌ور ('vindictive/resentful') and همگروه ('fellows/companions, allied'). Read as 'the victory of the resentful allied host' — i.e. the hostile allied army prevailed.
segments.s260-g81: خجسته نیا is a noun phrase with the adjective خجسته (‘auspicious/blessed’) placed before the noun نیا (‘ancestor’). را‌گر is a classical conditional particle equivalent to “if” (گر after را/this phrase), and اینست is an archaic copular expression ‘this is’. رای here means ‘counsel/plan’ and completes the clause: “If this is the counsel of the blessed ancestor.”
segments.s260-g82: سزد‌گر is a compacted conditional idea (سزد + گر) meaning ‘if it befits’ or ‘if that is fitting’. نداریم is the present first‑person plural ‘we do not have’ (negative of داشتن) and governs the following noun phrase رومی قبای ‘Roman robe’ (رومی = Roman, قبای = robe). Together: ‘if that is fitting, we should/shall have no Roman robe.’
segments.s260-g83: شوم is a subjunctive/jussive first‑person form meaning ‘I will become / let me become’ and here functions as the verb ‘I will put on’; جوشن is the direct object ‘cuirass/armor’. The clause then continues with و خود بیرون کنم: خود (‘myself’) is a reflexive/emphatic pronoun and بیرون کنم (بیرون + کنم) is a compound where کنم is the speaker’s volitive ‘I will/let me do’, so the whole says ‘I will don the cuirass and go out myself.’
segments.s260-g84: به is a directional preposition ‘to / toward’; می‌روی is the present stem ‘go’ and here introduces the movement toward the action that follows. پژمرده گلگلون is a resultive noun phrase (‘the withered / pale’ → ‘rosy‑cheeked’), and کنم is the main volitive verb ‘I will make / I will cause’. In this poetic compression the speaker promises both to go (به می‌روی) and to effect the change (پژمرده گلگلون کنم): ‘I will go there and make the withered become rosy‑cheeked.’
segments.s260-g85: چو آیم is a temporal subordinate clause ‘when I come’ (چو = ‘when’ + آیم = ‘I come’). جهان‌پهلوان را uses the object marker را to mark the beneficiary/target ‘the world‑champion,’ and به کار بیایم is an idiom meaning ‘to be of use / to come to (someone’s) aid’ (بیایم as a subjunctive/volitive ‘I will come/serve’ governed by به کار).
segments.s260-g86: بیایم here expresses the speaker’s resolve ‘I will come’ (jussive/first person), and کمربسته is a participial/adjectival compound meaning ‘girded (at the waist)’ placed before کارزار ‘battle’ — together ‘I will come girded for battle.’ Word order (adjective/participial phrase before noun) is normal in this poetic register.
segments.s260-g73: ببینی is a present/subjunctive (classical) form with the prefixed بـ used here to mean a future/imperative sense 'you will see' or 'see'. The pronoun تو follows the verb for emphasis (inverted word order). گوپال گودرز را is the definite object (two proper names listed in apposition) marked by را as the object marker.
segments.s260-g74: که introduces a relative/clausal clause ('who/that') referring back to the names. چون here means 'how' (introducing the manner). برنوردد is a classical present/subjunctive 3rd-singular (prefixed بـ + root نورد/نوردیدن) meaning 'overruns/overcomes'. همی is an emphatic/adverbial particle common in Ferdowsi (here strengthening the verb). مرز را is the definite object ('the border/frontier') with را.
segments.s260-g75: و simply connects this line to the previous. دیگر کجا is a rhetorical 'where else' (دیگر modifies کجا). ز is the poetic preposition 'from'. اختر نیک و بد literally 'star, good and bad' — i.e. 'auspicious and inauspicious stars' — where نیک و بد qualify اختر.
segments.s260-g76: همی is an emphatic/adverbial particle (here giving a continuous or emphatic sense). گردش چرخ را is the object 'the turning (گردش) of the wheel (چرخ)' marked by را. بشمرد is 3rd-person singular present/subjunctive (بـ + شمار/شمر) meaning 'he counts/reckons' and completes the clause; word order places the verb at the end as typical.
segments.s260-g77: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. پیش آید is a verb phrase meaning 'comes/occurs' (پیش as an adverbial element 'forth/forward' plus آید 'comes'). آن روزگار بهی is the temporal noun phrase 'that auspicious/good time' (بهی = good/auspicious).
segments.s260-g78: کند is 3rd-singular 'does/makes'. روی گیتی is a genitive phrase 'the face (روی) of the world (گیتی)'. ز introduces the agent/source 'from the Turks' (ز ترکان). تهی is the predicate adjective 'empty/devoid (of)', so the whole clause means 'it makes the world's face empty of the Turks.'
segments.s260-g79: چنین 'thus/so' introduces reported speech. گفت is the past tense 'said' and is followed by the subject بیژن (Bijan). به پیش پدر is a prepositional phrase 'in the presence of / before (his) father' (به + پیش + پدر). Word order places گفت before the subject as is common in epic narration.
segments.s260-g80: که here introduces the clause that follows (the reported words) and is immediately followed by the vocative ای (contracted in the manuscript as که‌ای): 'that: “O...”.' پهلوان is the vocative 'O champion/hero', جهان qualifies it as 'of the world' (پهلوان جهان = 'champion of the world'), and سر به سر is an idiomatic expression meaning 'entirely/throughout' placed after جهان for emphasis; the phrase is the beginning of Bijan's address.

sentences.s260-s21: If it should happen that he fears an ambush from the war-horsemen and the men of vengeance.
sentences.s260-s22: You must give me a thousand horsemen; choose for me those fit for the battlefield.
sentences.s260-s23: We will raise dust from their ambushes and scatter them before their leader (their 'moon').
sentences.s260-s24: Giv laughed at Bijan's words, and he lavished many praises upon Niu's son.
sentences.s260-s1: On the fourth day the army from the front advanced, and Bijan and Giv reached the very heart (of the fight).
sentences.s260-s2: They came before their father with all their garments torn, and they threw/scattered dust up toward the sky.
sentences.s260-s3: He said to him, 'O kây (vocative), Bab the tester of deeds, what is it that makes you stand there staring at us like this?'
sentences.s260-s4: On the fifth (day) this time rose to its height; night and day it became a teacher of rest (brought continuous peace).
sentences.s260-s9: At Lavān, where he saw so many young men slain, he saw that the Iranians' fortune had turned.
sentences.s260-s10: His heart (courage) has grown weary and has lost its way; he will no longer see the battlefield.
sentences.s260-s11: He must cast (his) eye upon his elders; his head is turned toward the high mountain.
sentences.s260-s12: A commander whose host is uncounted — he counts the stars and continually about the moon.
sentences.s260-s13: Know that there is no blood in his body; his warriors became abject from the fighting.
sentences.s260-s14: No wonder from the world‑wise Goudarz; for his own spirit is not upon this frontier.
sentences.s260-s15: It astonishes me, O father, because of you, that the lion of life seeks skill from you.
sentences.s260-s16: Two armies always keep their eyes on you; sharpen one (i.e. make one keen), sharpen your wits and kindle anger.
sentences.s260-s29: Let us take from him love and a pure bond; his father was the water of the sea, his mother the earth.
sentences.s260-s30: But you, O son, skilled in speech, do not thrust forward your tongue; do not open it wide.
sentences.s260-s31: For he is experienced and more knowing; he is the chief over this renowned host.
sentences.s260-s32: One who has profited in battle should not need a teacher for every one of his actions.
sentences.s260-s17: Now, when the world grows warm and the air grows bright, the army's war-tune begins to sound.
sentences.s260-s18: When this time of happiness passes, when the steel's face on the ground grows cold.
sentences.s260-s19: When the harp becomes sorrowful upon the spears, then the sword comes at the rear and the stone before.
sentences.s260-s20: who comes from the ranks to the front of the army; who brings and seizes this battlefield.
sentences.s260-s5: Neither did the sun behold the sword‑brandishing ranks, nor did a speck of dust rise into the air.
sentences.s260-s6: The horsemen were asleep and he himself was within; not a single man's blood moved in his veins.
sentences.s260-s7: In Iran, after the famed Rostam, there was no other horseman like Goudarz.
sentences.s260-s8: Thus, until he returned from the war, worn by that slaughter and by the hunger of the battlefield.
sentences.s260-s25: He said to the Creator, "I thank you; you gave me a son who knows goodness."
sentences.s260-s26: You gave him all his wit, and also strength and a sense of enmity; knowing every deed and seeking religion.
sentences.s260-s27: This brave young man returned to me; he was like a young champion.
sentences.s260-s28: Thus the pair said to me: “(The) male is the lion — that, if our child is not brave ...”
sentences.s260-s33: If our horsemen are burdened with baggage, the Turks are not decked out in color and ornament.
sentences.s260-s34: All are wretched and have turned their heads away; all have eyes full of blood and hearts weary.
sentences.s260-s35: This very trial (this matter of proving deeds) persistently demands that the Turks set foot in battle.
sentences.s260-s36: Then their rear remained far from the mountain; the resentful allied host gained the victory.
sentences.s260-s41: If this is the counsel of the blessed ancestor, then it is fitting that we have no Roman robe.
sentences.s260-s42: I will don a cuirass and go out myself; I will go there and make the withered become rosy‑cheeked.
sentences.s260-s43: When I come, I will be of use to the world‑champion; I will come girded for battle.
sentences.s260-s37: You will see Goupal and Goudarz, how they overrun the frontier.
sentences.s260-s38: And where else, from the auspicious and inauspicious stars, would he count the turning of the wheel?
sentences.s260-s39: When that auspicious time comes, it will make the face of the world empty of the Turks.
sentences.s260-s40: Thus Bijan said before his father, 'O champion of the world, wholly...'
